title: Cell encapsulation in inoculation beads for 3D growth experiments
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"zvyea","text":"This protocol provides an experimental model system to study bacterial competition in quasi-spherical three-dimensional bacterial colonies. To do so we encapsulate populations of two fluorescent Escherichia coli strains in inoculation droplets. The inoculation droplets grow when embedded in a hydrogel with nutrients and develop 3D colonies with well-defined sectors-like regions. The protocol can be extended and used with a wide range of different bacteria or – potentially – other cell types.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":195,"length":17}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"vp553","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"32hkh","text":"In this protocol, we describe a method for encapsulating bacterial cells within agarose beads following the procedure of Ref. [1]. These beads are especially useful as a starting point for bacterial growth within a three-dimensional medium. These – so called – inoculation beads are particularly suitable for investigating 3D colony expansion, conducting three-dimensional competition experiments, and for subsequent quantitative microscopy imaging.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":260,"length":18}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"wmt33","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"b1bty","text":"Here we describe the procedure for creating beads of 40-70 µm diameter, containing two distinct bacterial cell types at a 1:1 ratio. The protocol has been optimised for variations of  an E. coli B strain. However, it can be freely adapted for making beads of different sizes, of other ratios, with different strains or containing more than two strain types.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":187,"length":7}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"bctmm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"5elw0","text":"Following the steps detailed in this protocol leads to a batch of ~ 10 x 1 ml frozen inoculation bead stocks, storable long-term at -80°C. As detailed here, the beads can be inoculated inside an agar matrix (or other hydrogels) to obtain and investigate three-dimensional colonies.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"pygrx","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: One day prior to encapsulation, prepare overnight cultures of the desired bacterial strains. For each strain, transfer one colony initiated from a single cell into a 15 ml Falcon tube, containing 2 ml of Lysogenic broth (LB), using a wooden stick.
Incubate overnight at 37°C under constant shaking with the tube cap slightly open.
The following day, measure the optical densities at the wavelength of 600 nm (OD) of the overnight cultures.

Prepare a saturated 1:1 mixture, by mixing the overnight cultures in the ratio corresponding to their ratio of the measured ODs. NB: Cell strains may be mixed to any other ratio. The mixture may be diluted with LB if a lower concentration of cells per bead is desired.
Measure the OD of the resulting mixture to verify that the targeted OD value has been reached.
Meanwhile, put 15 ml of silicone oil in a 50 ml tube and preheat in a block heater at 55°C for 20 min.
Melt in a microwave a bottle of a pre-autoclaved sterile 2.5 % agarose in Milli-Q water solution. Make sure to shake vigorously for homogeneity and avoid boiling to minimize evaporation.
Transfer 500 µl of the newly melted agarose solution to a 2 ml (heated) tube in the block heater at 55°C.
Add 15 µl of pluronic acid to the 500 µl of agarose and let the agarose-pluronic solution cool to 55°C.
Transfer the tube to a shaker block heater set at 42°C.
Add 100 µl of the saturated 1:1 bacteria culture under continuous vortexing (1400 rpm).

Remove the silicone oil from the block heater and using pre-heated pipette tips (placed in an incubator at 60°C one day before bead-formation) add to it drop by drop 500 µl of this bacteria-agarose-pluronic acid mix. This step must be done fast to avoid untimely solidification of the agarose (at temperatures <25°C).
Vortex for 2 min at maximum speed the 50 ml tube containing the droplets.
Place the tube in a water-ice bath for an additional 10 min.

Centrifuge for 10 min at 550 g (room temperature). NB: Close the tube containing the silicone oil tidily so as not to spill it throughout the centrifuge. Remove the tube carefully from the centrifuge to not disturb the precipitated beads in the wall of the tube.
Remove the silicone oil by carefully removing the supernatant. NB: Remove the supernatant by pouring it through the opposite wall where the precipitated beads are located.
Add 5 ml of phosphate buffered saline (PBS).
Repeat step 3.10.

As described in section 2, the ratio and concentration of cells per bead are tuned by measuring the OD values. As a sanity check, we plate and count colony forming units. This part (section 4) can be conducted during the ice bath and centrifugation waiting times.
Serially dilute (by factor 10^6 - 10^7, as needed) the cell mixture previously created in step 2.2.
Take 100 µl from the last dilution and place it on LB plates with agar (1.5%).
Incubate the plates overnight, and count the number of CFUs for the various strains the following day.
Remove the silicone carefully from the surface by pipetting.
Repeat step 3.12.
Resuspend the washed beads by vortexing.
Let the bead-PBS solution flow through a set of strainers with mesh sizes of 70 µm, 50 µm and 40 µm placed on a 50 ml tube (Figure 1). NB: Substitute with strainers of other mesh sizes as needed.

Pour 5 ml of PBS through the strainers to ease the passage. The strainers can be reused up to three times by washing them with abundant demineralized water, soaping them for one night, and keeping them in ethanol. NB: More PBS can be used for rinsing, if needed.

To collect the selected beads, place the 40 µm strainer (containing the beads of 40 - 50 µm diameter) and the 50 µm strainer (containing the beads of 50 - 70 µm diameter) upside down on two new 50 ml sterile tubes (Figure 2).

Rinse with 6 ml of LB medium so the beads adsorped to the filter surface desorb (Figure 2). NB: Use a serological pipette for rinsing, as seen in Figure 2. More LB can be used for rinsing, for a lower bead concentration in the solution.

For long-term storage, aliquote the bead-medium solution in volumes of 500 µl in 2 ml vials, and mix with 500 µl of 50% glycerol-solution (11 vials pr. batch), and store at -80°C. NB: Pipette each volume separately, and vortex the bead-medium solution before filling each vial, to ensure that the bead per ml concentration is constant across all vials.
Melt bottles of 20 ml millipore water with 0.625% agar by repeated cycles of heating (in the microwave) and shaking to ensure homogeneity and minimise evaporation and ageing.
When the bottle is at 55°C, add M63 salt and supplements to a final M63+glu with 0.5% agar.
Transfer 1 ml of this solution to a pre-heated tube in the (non-shaking) block heater (55°C).
Thaw on the bench a frozen vial of bead-medium solution for 5 min until melted.

To minimise time spent in high-concentration glycerol, dilute the bead-medium solution in LB. Use 20-500 µl (as needed, aiming for 10 inoculation beads per well) of the bead-medium solution with 1 ml of LB. NB: The concentration of beads per ml frozen stock (FS) should be constant within one batch, but strongly varies between batches (between several hundred to several thousand beads per 1 ml FS). It is recommended to conduct a few trial embedments to determine the concentration of beads in a batch, and use the results to tune the amount of FS necessary to obtain 10 beads per well.
Meanwhile, supplement the agar-medium solution with 10 µl of a 1 M stock of KNO 3 and 30 µg/ml kanamycin.
Quickly hereafter, add to the agar-medium solution 50 µl of the diluted bead-medium solution. NB: Use large pipette tips whenever transferring beads, to prevent damage. Do not rinse pipettes.
Vortex and pour the entire tube content into a glass-bottomed culture well.
Leave to solidify for a few min on the bench before incubation.
Incubate (upside-down) at 37°C for at least 5 hours.

If the cell-agarose-pluronic acid solution mixture solidifies when transferring to the warm silicone oil, you will need to use warmer pipettes and keep them warm before using, e.g. with a calorimeter, and do the procedure faster.

The final product resulting from following this protocol step by step is a batch of 10 x 1 ml inoculation bead stocks, storable for the long term with glycerol at -80°C. The frozen beads stocks can be used at any time after melting. The three-dimensional colonies can be visualised by inoculating the beads inside an agar matrix (or other hydrogels).
Genetic mixing and demixing on expanding spherical frontiers by Alba García Vázquez, Namiko Mitarai, Liselotte Jauffred https://dx.doi.org/10.1101/2023.07.07.548058

Figure 1. Sketch protocol of cell encapsulation in inoculation beads: i) 1:1 mixture of fluorescent E.coli B strain with 2.5 % agarose and pluronic acid. ii) Inoculation beads are formed when the mixture is added drop by drop into warm silicone oil. iii) After vortexing, the tube containing the droplets is placed in a water-ice bath to cool down. iv) The tube is centrifuged. v) The beads are size-fractionated between 70-40 µm. vi) Inoculation beads stored long-term at -80°C. Figure 2. Size-fractionating the inoculation beads: (left of the arrow) The beads are size-fractionated with 40 - 70 µm diameter strainers. (Right of the arrow) The beads adsorbed to the filter surface are desorbed when rinsing with LB. Figure 3. Sketch of embedment of inoculation beads in 0.5% agar: i) Frozen stock is thawed on the bench until melted. ii) Transfer 20-500 µl into 1 ml of LB (to minimise the time spent in high-concentration glycerol), aiming for 10 beads/well. iii) After supplementing the agar-medium solution with 10 µl of a 1 M stock of KNO3 and 30 µg/ml kanamycin, 50 µl of the diluted bead-medium are added to the agar-medium solution. iv) Vortex and pour the entire tube content into a glass-bottomed culture well. v) After solidification of the agar, incubate the plate for at least 5 hours at 37°C.
